blocks.content: He stood up from the table.

Walked to the window. Stood with his back to her and his hands at his sides — and she could see it happening in the line of his shoulders, the thing he'd been holding for nine days trying to find somewhere to put it.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I keep thinking about the book on my nightstand.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I'd gotten through the whole second trimester chapter. I had tabs on names. I'd been looking at — I had a list. Of things we'd need. And I just — I don't know where to put any of it now. I don't know what to do with having wanted something that much and having it —

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I didn't know I could want something that much. I didn't know I'd gotten there already. And now it's just gone and everyone keeps telling me it's common and it's normal and the body does what the body does and I know that. I know all of that. But it doesn't —

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: It doesn't feel like a statistic. It feels like losing someone.
She put her arms around him from behind — the way he used to hold her at sixteen, at the bonfire, that particular way — and pressed her face to his back and held on.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: It is losing someone. It counts. What we felt is real. What we lost is real.
He put his hands over her arms.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: You are not your father. You are not his silence. This — what you just did — this is the opposite of what he did.
He was quiet for a long time.

Then he turned around and held her back, and she let him, and they stood in the middle of their small Los Angeles apartment and grieved together the way the counseling had prepared them to — not alone, not in parallel, but the same thing at the same time.
It was the hardest night of their marriage.
It was also the night the marriage became something unbreakable.
